questions.question: What question matters most right after choosing a leader-follower design?
options: What commit rule, read routing policy, and failover behavior define the real guarantee?
Which diagram color makes followers easy to spot?
How many services can be placed in one box on the sketch?
Whether the database vendor uses the word cluster in marketing.
questions.explanation: Leader-follower only becomes meaningful once commit, read, and failover semantics are clear.

questions.question: Why can follower reads be dangerous on critical paths?
options: Because lag can make the system return old state even when the write has already been acknowledged elsewhere.
Because followers cannot handle read traffic.
Because leaders are always cheaper for reads.
Because replicas never expose version information.
questions.explanation: Follower lag is a product-visible consistency decision.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of waiting for a follower acknowledgement before confirming writes?
options: You improve failover durability but usually pay higher write latency and more sensitivity to replica health.
You eliminate the need for observability.
You make split brain impossible without fencing.
You reduce replication bandwidth to zero.
questions.explanation: Stronger durability usually costs latency and operational flexibility.

questions.question: Which metric best detects whether follower reads are becoming unsafe?
options: Follower freshness age or version lag on the entities served from replicas.
The number of diagram arrows in the lesson notes.
Total CPU time on the leader only.
Weekly hiring headcount.
questions.explanation: Freshness metrics connect replica lag to actual read safety.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of blind automatic promotion after a leader failure?
options: A stale follower can become leader and lose acknowledged or expected writes if promotion is not fenced and validated.
Read traffic becomes permanently unavailable everywhere.
Followers stop replicating forever by design.
Caches automatically delete the working set.
questions.explanation: Promotion safety is about avoiding stale leadership and split brain.

questions.question: When is routing reads only to the leader often justified?
options: When the read path is correctness-critical and stale results would cause harmful user or policy outcomes.
When the system has more than one availability zone.
When diagrams need fewer arrows.
When writes are rare in development.
questions.explanation: Critical read correctness can justify higher cost and latency.

questions.question: Why should failover logs include leader epoch and replica commit position?
options: Because operators need evidence that the promoted node was current enough and properly fenced.
Because those fields make dashboards look more advanced.
Because they replace the need for health checks.
Because follower lag no longer matters once a node is promoted.
questions.explanation: Those details make failover decisions auditable and debuggable.

questions.question: Which answer sounds most senior?
options: We will use replicas for cheap reads, but only after separating critical versus convenience paths and defining promotion safety.
We will add followers because read scaling is always free.
We will let any healthy replica become leader whenever needed.
We will call the system strongly consistent because there is a primary node.
questions.explanation: The strongest answer ties topology choices to guarantees and failure handling.
